documents.title: Development of an artificial intelligence (AI) marine scientific research model: The case of marine science and technology policy and its legitimate practice in Taiwan

problem_and_intent.geographic_scope: Taiwan with comparison to EU, United States, Japan, and Korea
problem_and_intent.temporal_scope: long-term governance and sustainable ocean management
problem_and_intent.problem_statement: Taiwan is expanding AI use in marine science, but legal and governance gaps around data legitimacy, security, and cross-agency coordination can limit trustworthy deployment.
problem_and_intent.primary_goal: Develop a governance-oriented AI marine scientific research model for legally compliant and credible marine data use.
secondary_goals: Support biodiversity monitoring and underwater exploration
Align marine AI development with international sustainability frameworks

data_source: marine exploration and ecological survey project
government and research marine data collection
data_characteristics.temporal_dimension: ongoing programmatic use under climate change
data_characteristics.spatial_dimension: national marine domain with international policy benchmarking

innovation_type: cross-domain transfer from AI to marine governance
governance model for legitimate AI-enabled marine research

outputs_and_deliverables.decision_support_role: Provides a governance framework for deciding how marine AI systems and datasets can be collected, inspected, and used legitimately.

ethical_considerations: responsible AI use in marine science
technical_limitations: need for reliable data handling protocols
environmental_considerations: climate change impacts on marine biodiversity

end_users: public agencies responsible for marine data governance
stakeholders_and_ecosystem.adoption_context: National governance and research administration context.

implementation_and_deployment.scalability: high for governance adoption, moderate for technical integration
operational_requirements: coordination between research and policy actors
implementation_and_deployment.cost_feasibility: Moderate, with nontrivial institutional coordination costs.

not_addressed_but_relevant: operational field validation on coral-specific indicators
evidence_snippets: Highlights Taiwan's integration of AI into biodiversity monitoring, underwater exploration, and environmental analysis.
Addresses legal challenges in intellectual property, data protection, and national security.
Develops a governance model using data collection policies, legitimating measures, and inspection protocols.

documents.title: Marine Corps Artificial Intelligence Strategy
documents.citation: United States Marine Corps Artificial Intelligence Strategy, August 2024

problem_and_intent.subdomain: military decision-support and enterprise AI adoption
problem_and_intent.geographic_scope: United States Marine Corps and partner ecosystem
problem_and_intent.problem_statement: The Marine Corps wants faster and better decision-making through AI, but faces misalignment with mission objectives, skills gaps, scale-up challenges, and legacy governance barriers.
problem_and_intent.primary_goal: Integrate responsible AI across the Marine Corps to create decision advantage.
secondary_goals: Improve AI competency across the force
Deploy AI from enterprise systems to the tactical edge

data_source: Marine Corps operational and business systems
data_characteristics.spatial_dimension: enterprise through tactical edge in contested environments

outputs_and_deliverables.decision_support_role: Sets conditions for AI-enabled decision advantage across warfighting and business operations.
performance_indicators: reducing minutes to seconds in decision-making
ability to deploy from enterprise to tactical edge

legal_regulatory_constraints: policy development constraints in defense context
technical_limitations: data management is the largest challenge

stakeholders_and_ecosystem.adoption_context: Service-wide military modernization and warfighting support.

implementation_and_deployment.cost_feasibility: High strategic value but resource intensive.

evidence_snippets: Provides a framework for integrating AI across all levels of the Marine Corps to support better and faster decision-making.
Identifies misalignment with mission objectives, AI competency gaps, scale challenges, and legacy governance barriers.
Deploying AI from enterprise to the edge depends on efficient data management, labeling, and curation.

documents.title: Key Policy Asks for Coral Reefs: Accelerating the Decade of Action #ForCoral

problem_and_intent.subdomain: coral reef protection, restoration, and governance
problem_and_intent.geographic_scope: global with national and regional implementation pathways
problem_and_intent.temporal_scope: urgent action through 2025 and the remainder of the SDG and GBF implementation horizon
problem_and_intent.problem_statement: Coral reefs are under urgent pressure from climate change and localized anthropogenic threats, and policy action is needed to halt degradation and build long-term ecological and socio-economic resilience.
problem_and_intent.primary_goal: Accelerate coordinated action for coral reef protection, restoration, monitoring, and equitable governance.
secondary_goals: Reduce land-based pollution and fisheries pressures
Strengthen standardized monitoring and restoration practice
Integrate coral reefs into climate and biodiversity policy

technical_components: restoration paired with local threat reduction

data_type: biological, physical, social, cultural, and economic variables
data_characteristics.temporal_dimension: tracking conditions and targets over space and time
data_characteristics.spatial_dimension: local, national, regional, and global reef systems

outputs_and_deliverables.decision_support_role: Defines the actions and indicators governments and reef managers should use to prioritize coral policy and management.
performance_indicators: expansion of protected and effectively managed reef areas
restoration impacts measured at ecosystem level
integration of coral reefs into climate and biodiversity plans

legal_regulatory_constraints: need for enforceable regulations on pollution, fisheries, and land use
ethical_considerations: rights of indigenous peoples and local communities
technical_limitations: separate climate and biodiversity institutions can weaken coordination
need for standardized monitoring and data handling

stakeholders_and_ecosystem.adoption_context: Global policy advocacy translated into national and regional coral management.

integration_requirements: integration into NDCs, NAPs, and NBSAPs
linkage across climate, biodiversity, and fisheries institutions
implementation_and_deployment.cost_feasibility: Potentially costly to implement broadly, but framed as necessary for long-term ecological and socio-economic resilience.

not_addressed_but_relevant: specific AI or analytics architectures for monitoring
site-level benchmark performance thresholds for automated classification
evidence_snippets: Calls for meaningful water quality targets and strong regulation on pollution and watersheds.
Advances standardized monitoring and use of Healthy Coral Reef Indicators over space and time.
Supports citizen science monitoring networks and integration into NDCs, NAPs, and NBSAPs.

documents.title: Multi-label classification for multi-temporal, multi-spatial coral reef condition monitoring using vision foundation model with adapter learning

problem_and_intent.subdomain: coral reef condition monitoring with foundation models
problem_and_intent.geographic_scope: Koh Tao, Thailand with cross-site and cross-season transfer emphasis
problem_and_intent.problem_statement: Manual underwater coral monitoring is labor intensive, inconsistent, and hard to generalize across sites and seasons, while conventional deep models struggle with complex underwater imagery.
problem_and_intent.primary_goal: Improve multi-label coral reef condition classification using a parameter-efficient vision foundation model approach.
secondary_goals: Improve generalizability across temporal and spatial settings
Support conservation and management with better monitoring

innovation_type: application of foundation models to coral reef monitoring

outputs_and_deliverables.decision_support_role: Produces automated coral condition labels that can support monitoring, conservation, and management decisions.

ethical_considerations: dependency on labeling standards and human interpretation quality
technical_limitations: need for large compute in full fine-tuning
generalization challenges across regions and seasons

stakeholders_and_ecosystem.adoption_context: Prototype monitoring workflow that could plug into coral survey programs.

implementation_and_deployment.cost_feasibility: Moderate, improved by LoRA but still dependent on compute and curation.

not_addressed_but_relevant: water quality, fisheries, or restoration policy instruments
evidence_snippets: Uses underwater surveys at 15 dive sites at Koh Tao, Thailand, labeled to universal standards used in citizen science-based conservation programs.
DINOv2-LoRA achieved a 64.77% match ratio compared with 60.34% for the best conventional model.
The model is proposed as a tool for monitoring, conserving, and managing coral reef ecosystems.

documents.title: Assessing Derawan Island's Coral Reefs over Two Decades: A Machine Learning Classification Perspective

problem_and_intent.subdomain: coral reef habitat monitoring with machine learning
problem_and_intent.problem_statement: Coral reef habitats on Derawan Island are changing over time and need accurate, multi-resolution monitoring to inform conservation strategy.
problem_and_intent.primary_goal: Assess two decades of coral reef habitat change using machine learning classification on multispectral imagery.
secondary_goals: Compare RF, SVM, and CART across data resolutions
Identify the most accurate classification approach
Support ecological monitoring and conservation planning

innovation_type: comparative model selection for coral habitat mapping
integration of multiple spatial resolutions for long-term monitoring

outputs_and_deliverables.decision_support_role: Provides spatial evidence on coral reef change and an empirically preferred classifier for monitoring programs.
performance_indicators: RF accuracy of 78.28% with multispectral aerial photos
reef area reduced to 16 hectares from 2003 to 2011 before a slight later increase

technical_limitations: accuracy depends on field and image data quality
dataset size not ideal for deep learning
environmental_considerations: coral reefs threatened by climate change

stakeholders_and_ecosystem.adoption_context: Operationalizable monitoring and mapping workflow for site-level coral assessment.

integration_requirements: access to multispectral and aerial imagery
implementation_and_deployment.cost_feasibility: Moderate and potentially favorable for programs already using remote sensing.

not_addressed_but_relevant: formal governance and legal compliance layers
evidence_snippets: Uses Landsat 9, Landsat 7, Sentinel-2, and multispectral aerial photos to assess coral habitats over 2003, 2011, and 2021.
RF was the most accurate classifier, reaching 78.28% accuracy with multispectral aerial photos.
Monitoring is positioned as a way to inform conservation strategies in an ecologically sensitive coral reef area.

documents.title: Microplastic as an invisible threat to the coral reefs: Sources, toxicity mechanisms, policy intervention, and the way forward
documents.citation: Rahman et al., Journal of Hazardous Materials 454 (2023) 131522

problem_and_intent.subdomain: microplastic impacts on coral reefs and policy response
problem_and_intent.temporal_scope: long-term and ongoing global pollution pressure
problem_and_intent.problem_statement: Microplastic pollution threatens coral reefs, can bioaccumulate through food chains, and remains under-addressed in coral reef policy and research.
problem_and_intent.primary_goal: Synthesize knowledge on sources, distribution, toxicity mechanisms, policy intervention, and future directions for microplastics in coral reef systems.
secondary_goals: Explain risks to coral and human health

data_type: global evidence on coral reef microplastics

innovation_type: integrated synthesis of science and policy intervention for microplastic threats
resource_requirements: policy attention to critical coral reef areas

outputs_and_deliverables.decision_support_role: Provides policy-relevant synthesis on why microplastic mitigation matters for coral reefs and where evidence and governance gaps remain.
performance_indicators: identifies global variation in microplastic distribution and sources
connects coral impacts to human health concerns
critiques existing policy and offers mitigation recommendations

legal_regulatory_constraints: need for stronger policy intervention on plastic pollution
ethical_considerations: human health impacts through food-chain exposure
ecological harm to vulnerable reef systems
technical_limitations: limited empirical coverage in some coral reef regions
knowledge gaps on distribution, fate, and effects

institutional_context: global coral reef governance and research landscape
stakeholders_and_ecosystem.adoption_context: Evidence base for priority setting and pollution-related coral policy design.

implementation_and_deployment.scalability: high as policy evidence, not a deployable technical system
integration_requirements: translation into pollution regulation and monitoring agendas
linkage to coral reef management plans
implementation_and_deployment.cost_feasibility: High feasibility as a policy evidence input; downstream implementation costs depend on chosen mitigation measures.

evidence_snippets: Summarizes the global distribution and source of microplastics within coral reefs.
Critically analyzes existing policy and further recommendations to mitigate microplastic contamination on corals.
Highlights mechanisms affecting coral and human health and calls for research focused on critical coral reef areas.
